intro: Calculate exactly how much to feed your cat per day based on their weight and life stage. Uses the veterinary RER formula to estimate daily calorie needs, then converts to grams of dry or wet food for easy portion measuring.
howItWorks: Resting Energy Requirement (RER) = 70 × weight(kg)^0.75. The RER is multiplied by a life stage factor: kittens 2.5×, indoor adults 1.0×, outdoor/active adults 1.4×, senior cats 1.1×, pregnant/nursing 1.6×, overweight-prone 0.8×. Dry food equivalent assumes approximately 350 kcal/100g; wet food approximately 90 kcal/100g — check your specific brand.
interpretationGuide: Cats are obligate carnivores with specific nutritional requirements — they need taurine, arachidonic acid, and preformed vitamin A from animal sources. Always use cat-specific food. Healthy weight: ribs palpable with light pressure, visible waist from above, slight abdominal tuck. Overweight cats should lose weight slowly (0.5% body weight per week maximum) to avoid hepatic lipidosis.

faqs.question: How often should I feed my cat?
faqs.answer: Adult cats can be fed once or twice daily, or have food available all day (free feeding), though free feeding often leads to overeating. Twice daily feeding is most common. Kittens need 3–4 meals per day until 6 months old.

faqs.question: Should I feed wet or dry cat food?
faqs.answer: Both can be nutritionally complete. Wet food has 70–80% moisture and is beneficial for cats prone to urinary issues or kidney disease. Dry food is convenient and may be better for dental health. Many vets recommend a mix. If mixing, adjust portions so total daily calories match the calculator output.

faqs.question: How many calories are in cat food?
faqs.answer: Dry kibble typically contains 300–400 kcal per 100g. Wet food is 70–120 kcal per 100g (it's mostly water). Treats are often 300–500 kcal/100g. Always check the packaging — calorie density varies significantly between brands and formulations.

faqs.question: Why do indoor cats need fewer calories than outdoor cats?
faqs.answer: Indoor cats are less active — they don't hunt, explore territory, or regulate body temperature outdoors. Their metabolism adjusts accordingly. An indoor cat on the same portion as an outdoor cat will gradually gain weight. Neutered indoor cats are the most prone to obesity.

faqs.question: My cat seems hungry all the time — should I feed more?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. Cats often beg out of habit or boredom rather than hunger. Check the body condition score first — if you can feel ribs easily and see a waist, they're at a healthy weight. Hunger can also signal hyperthyroidism or diabetes in older cats, so consult a vet if a senior cat suddenly increases food seeking.

faqs.question: How do I help my cat lose weight safely?
faqs.answer: Reduce calories by 20–30% from maintenance needs and use the 'overweight-prone' life stage setting. Aim for 0.5% body weight loss per week. Never starve a cat — rapid weight loss causes hepatic lipidosis (fatty liver disease), which can be fatal. Increase play and activity alongside dietary changes.

faqs.question: Do neutered cats need less food?
faqs.answer: Yes. Neutering reduces metabolism and energy requirements by approximately 25–30%. If you don't reduce portions after neutering, your cat will gain weight. Use the 'adult-indoor' setting (1.0× RER) for neutered indoor cats, which is already adjusted for lower activity.

faqs.question: What is taurine and why do cats need it?
faqs.answer: Taurine is an essential amino acid for cats that they cannot synthesise themselves — they must get it from food. Deficiency causes dilated cardiomyopathy (heart disease) and blindness. All commercial cat foods are supplemented with taurine; this is why dogs should not eat cat food long-term and why cats should never eat dog food.

faqs.question: Can I feed my cat raw food?
faqs.answer: Raw diets can be fed to cats but require careful formulation to ensure nutritional completeness and minimize pathogen risk. Commercial raw foods are generally safer than home-prepared. If going raw, consult a veterinary nutritionist. This calculator works for raw food if you know the calorie density.

faqs.question: How much water should my cat drink?
faqs.answer: Cats fed dry food need to drink more water — approximately 60ml per kg of body weight per day. A 4kg cat needs ~240ml. Many cats are chronically underhydrated on dry-only diets, which can contribute to kidney disease. Use wet food, a water fountain, or multiple water bowls to encourage drinking.

faqs.question: When should I switch from kitten to adult cat food?
faqs.answer: Switch at 12 months for most cats, or 18–24 months for large breeds (Maine Coon, Norwegian Forest Cat, Ragdoll). Kitten food is higher in calories and protein to support growth — keeping cats on it too long promotes weight gain.

faqs.question: Should senior cats eat differently?
faqs.answer: Senior cats (10+ years) have variable needs. Many need fewer calories as they become less active. Some lose weight and muscle mass and actually need more protein (around 40–50% of dry matter). Senior cat foods are formulated for both scenarios — choose based on your cat's current weight trend and consult your vet annually.
